would benefit the workingman.


This charge against the banking
interests was at first made by a few
of the leaders of the working classes,
but it spread until practically the
whole element of labor in Spokane
felt that an injustice was being done
by allowing the banks to hold the
cash. The other side was Just as
DESTROYS that the banks should keep
the money and A. l.. Davis, a stock
holder in the LIE Roi mine, where
Turner also made his fortune,
brought an action for Injunction
against the city authorities to pre-
vent the commencement of the water
system construction work. The firm
of attorneys Mr.. Davis engaged was
known by the style of Turner.
Graves & McKinstry. The senior
partner was George Turner. now
candidate for governor on the demo-
cratic ticket; the next member was
Frank Graves, now one of Turner's
campaign managers; McKinstrey has
passed out Of the history of Spo-
kane.


The commencement of the injunc-
tion action brought things to a focus
as far as the werkingmen were con-
cerned, and they called a mass meet
ing at Auditorium hall for wednes-
day night. November 20. Vigorous
speeches unmasking the real motives
Of the banking interests were made.
The meeting was entirely orderly.
The day following was marked by
parades Of the workingmen in the
